title: Final Array State After K Multiplication Operations II
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: Pareto-optimal challenge: the single C++ solution is the recommended fast + lean proposal, the speed extreme, and the memory extreme. Separate solution-runtime or solution-memory files would be coincident, so they are intentionally omitted.

variants.languageReason: The workload is heap maintenance, sorting, and modular exponentiation over n <= 10000 with k up to 1e9. C++ gives native integer arithmetic, contiguous vector<uint64_t> storage, branch-light heap/sort operations, and no managed/interpreted overhead. Packing (value, index) into one key is valid because index < 2^14 and simulated values remain <= initialMax <= 1e9.
variants.approachJustification: Simulate the exact min-by-value-then-index operations only while min * multiplier <= initialMax. Once min * multiplier > initialMax, operations cycle through sorted (value, index) order; distribute remaining operations as full rounds plus leading extras and apply modular exponentiation.
variants.timeComplexity: O(n + s log n + n log n + log k), s <= n * ceil(log_multiplier(initialMax)) and <= about 30n for multiplier >= 2

reasoningSummary: The direct simulation prefix is short because every simulated update stays <= the initial maximum. When the minimum times multiplier exceeds that maximum, the multiplied item moves behind all unprocessed items, so the operation order becomes a repeated sorted (value, index) cycle. Remaining operations are distributed by quotient/remainder, and huge powers are handled modulo 1e9+7.
timeComplexity: O(n + s log n + n log n + log k)
